# Tenancy Inventory and Evidence Guidance ## Overview In tenancy deposit disputes, the quality and reliability of evidence are critical. Deposit adjudicators assess evidence provided by landlords and tenants to determine whether deductions from a tenancy deposit are justified. Strong documentary and photographic evidence significantly increases the likelihood of a successful claim or defence. Good record keeping throughout the tenancy can also help prevent disputes before adjudication becomes necessary. --- # Importance of Inventories ## Check-In and Check-Out Inventory Reports Signed check-in and check-out inventory reports are among the most important forms of evidence in tenancy deposit disputes. These reports allow adjudicators to compare: * the condition of the property at the start of the tenancy * the condition at the end of the tenancy Without reliable comparative evidence, claims relating to: * cleaning * damage * missing items * gardening * redecoration * wear and tear may fail or be significantly weakened. --- ## Signed Reports Signed inventories carry greater evidential weight because they demonstrate that both parties had the opportunity to review and agree the property condition. Unsigned reports are harder to verify and may reduce the reliability of the evidence during adjudication. Reports should ideally: * be signed by all parties * clearly describe the condition of each room and item * include detailed observations * record cleanliness and existing defects * contain supporting photographs --- # Landlord Responsibilities Landlords and letting agents should: * prepare accurate check-in inventories at tenancy start * complete check-out inspections promptly after tenancy end * retain copies of signed agreements and reports * maintain written records throughout the tenancy * gather evidence supporting any proposed deductions Landlords should also retain: * invoices * estimates * receipts * inspection reports * correspondence with tenants Where deductions are claimed, the landlord carries the burden of proof. The landlord must demonstrate: 1. the original condition of the property 2. deterioration beyond fair wear and tear 3. financial loss suffered 4. that the deduction claimed is reasonable --- # Tenant Responsibilities Tenants should: * review inventories carefully before signing * record disagreements or missing information * retain copies of signed documents * document property condition during the tenancy and at move-out * communicate repair or maintenance concerns in writing where possible Tenants may also submit their own evidence during dispute resolution, including: * photographs * emails or text messages * witness statements * receipts for cleaning or repairs --- # Photographic and Video Evidence ## Date-Stamped Images Date-stamped photographs and videos are highly valuable evidence in deposit disputes. “Before and after” images allow adjudicators to assess: * cleaning standards * damage * deterioration * missing items * property condition over time Images should: * clearly show the alleged issue * be high quality and readable * include explanations or descriptions where necessary * identify the relevant room, item, or surface Electronic originals are generally preferred over photocopies or low-quality reproductions. --- # Periodic Property Inspection Reports Mid-tenancy inspection reports can support claims by showing: * ongoing property condition * maintenance concerns * tenant compliance with tenancy obligations * progression of deterioration or damage These reports do not need to be as detailed as inventories but may provide useful supporting evidence. --- # Supporting Documentary Evidence Additional evidence commonly used in adjudication includes: * signed tenancy agreements * invoices and receipts * repair quotations and estimates * rent account statements * contractor reports * witness statements * correspondence between parties Communications such as emails, text messages, and written notices may help adjudicators establish timelines, agreements, repair reporting, or admissions of liability. --- # Dispute Resolution and

ongoing property condition * maintenance concerns * tenant compliance with tenancy obligations * progression of deterioration or damage These reports do not need to be as detailed as inventories but may provide useful supporting evidence. --- # Supporting Documentary Evidence Additional evidence commonly used in adjudication includes: * signed tenancy agreements * invoices and receipts * repair quotations and estimates * rent account statements * contractor reports * witness statements * correspondence between parties Communications such as emails, text messages, and written notices may help adjudicators establish timelines, agreements, repair reporting, or admissions of liability. --- # Dispute Resolution and Evidence Assessment Deposit disputes are assessed by independent adjudicators using the evidence submitted by both parties. The strength, consistency, and completeness of inventories and supporting documentation can therefore significantly influence the outcome of a tenancy deposit dispute. --- # Key Principle Reliable signed inventories supported by clear photographic evidence are among the strongest forms of evidence in tenancy deposit adjudication and are often decisive in disputes concerning cleaning, damage, and fair wear and tear.

A landlord who wishes to make deductions from a tenancy deposit must normally show evidence of the property’s condition at both the start and end of the tenancy. Signed inventories, inspection reports, photographs, videos, and written records are commonly used for this purpose. ## Role of Inventories in Deposit Disputes An inventory is a written record describing the condition and contents of a property when the tenant moves in. It may include descriptions of rooms, fixtures, furniture, decoration, appliances, and levels of cleanliness. Inventories are important because they provide a baseline against which the property’s condition at the end of the tenancy can be compared. Deposit protection schemes consider inventories and checkout reports to be strong evidence in disputes involving: - cleaning deductions - property damage - missing items - decoration issues - furniture condition Where no inventory exists, it may become difficult for a landlord to prove that deterioration occurred during the tenancy. ## Check-In Reports A check-in report is normally completed when the tenant moves into the property. It records the property’s condition at the beginning of the tenancy and is often linked to the inventory. For evidence purposes, check-in reports are strongest when: - signed by both landlord and tenant - completed before or on move-in day - supported by photographs or videos - detailed and room-specific Tenants should review the report carefully and raise any disagreements or missing issues immediately. ## Check-Out Reports A checkout report records the condition of the property after the tenant leaves. Deposit schemes compare the checkout report with the original inventory and check-in evidence to identify any changes. Tenants are encouraged to attend checkout inspections where possible so they can: - confirm observations - challenge inaccurate notes - provide immediate explanations - ensure comments are recorded fairly A checkout report completed solely by the landlord or agent may sometimes be considered less reliable than one prepared independently. # Landlord Deposit Claims and Proof Requirements ## Overview Landlords and letting agents may seek deductions from a tenancy deposit where they have suffered financial loss caused by breaches of the tenancy agreement. Common claim categories include: * rent arrears * cleaning costs * repairs and redecoration * replacement of damaged or missing items * garden maintenance Any deduction must be reasonable, proportionate, and supported by evidence. Adjudicators assess whether the landlord has demonstrated entitlement to deductions using documentary and photographic evidence. --- # Burden of Proof In tenancy deposit disputes, the burden of proof rests with the landlord. The tenancy deposit legally remains the tenant’s money unless the landlord can demonstrate: 1. a breach of the tenancy agreement 2. deterioration beyond fair wear and tear 3. financial loss suffered 4. that the proposed deduction is reasonable Without sufficient evidence, disputed amounts may be returned to the tenant. --- # Reasonable vs Unreasonable Claims ## Reasonable Claims Reasonable claims compensate the landlord for actual financial loss caused by the tenant. Adjudicators may award: * repair costs * cleaning costs * partial replacement costs * compensation for reduced lifespan or value Where replacement is unnecessary, repair or cleaning may be considered more appropriate. --- ## Unreasonable Claims Landlords must not seek to improve the property at the tenant’s expense. This principle is known as betterment. Examples of unreasonable claims may include: * charging full replacement cost for an old carpet * replacing lower-quality items with premium alternatives * charging for professional cleaning where cleaning defects already existed at check-in Claims must reflect the age, quality, and condition of the item at tenancy start. --- # Fair Wear and Tear Fair wear and tear refers to deterioration caused by ordinary use over time. The House of Lords defines fair wear and tear as: > “reasonable use of the premises by the tenant and the ordinary operation of natural forces” Adjudicators commonly consider: * length of tenancy * number and age of occupants * quality and lifespan of items * ordinary household usage Examples of likely fair wear and tear include: * minor carpet wear * faded furnishings * light wall scuffs * gradual appliance deterioration --- # Wear and Tear vs Damage Adjudicators distinguish between ordinary deterioration and tenant-caused damage. ## Examples More Likely to Be Fair Wear and Tear * light carpet marks from normal use * minor scuffing * gradual fading * ordinary ageing of fixtures ## Examples More Likely to Be Tenant Damage * nail varnish spills * iron burns * negligent breakage * severe staining * deliberate or careless damage Claims should reflect whether deterioration resulted from ordinary use or negligence. --- # Evidence Expectations for Landlords Strong evidence is essential for successful deposit claims. Adjudicators rely entirely on submitted evidence and do not usually visit the property. --- # Core Evidence Types ## Signed Tenancy Agreement The tenancy agreement establishes: * tenant obligations * cleaning responsibilities * maintenance terms * contractual breaches This document forms the foundation of most claims. --- ## Signed Check-In and Check-Out Reports Comparative inventories are among the strongest forms of evidence. These reports allow adjudicators to compare: * property condition at move-in * property condition at move-out Signed reports generally carry greater evidential weight than unsigned documents. Claims for cleaning or damage may fail where reliable comparative evidence is missing. --- ## Periodic Inspection Reports Mid-tenancy inspection reports may support claims by documenting: * developing deterioration * maintenance issues * ongoing property condition These reports can strengthen timelines and corroborate other evidence. --- ## Communication Records Emails, letters, and messages may help

Claims for cleaning or damage may fail where reliable comparative evidence is missing. --- ## Periodic Inspection Reports Mid-tenancy inspection reports may support claims by documenting: * developing deterioration * maintenance issues * ongoing property condition These reports can strengthen timelines and corroborate other evidence. --- ## Communication Records Emails, letters, and messages may help establish: * repair reporting * admissions of liability * agreements between parties * timelines of events Written communication can support or weaken responsibility arguments. --- ## Invoices, Estimates, and Receipts Financial evidence should support all monetary claims. Adjudicators assess whether costs are proportionate to the damage demonstrated. --- ## Photographs and Videos Date-stamped visual evidence is highly persuasive. Landlords may perform cleaning themselves and support the claim using contractor quotations showing comparable market costs. --- ## Repair and Redecoration Claims Repair or redecoration claims should be proportionate and account for: * fair wear and tear * item lifespan * age and quality * existing deterioration Full replacement may only be justified where damage is extensive or beyond economic repair. --- # Key Principle Successful tenancy deposit claims depend on clear, comparative, and proportionate evidence. Adjudicators assess whether the landlord has demonstrated genuine financial loss beyond fair wear and tear using reliable inventories, photographs, invoices, tenancy agreements, and communication records.

# Tenancy Deposit Deductions: Reasonable Claims and Landlord Proof Obligations ## Overview A tenancy deposit remains the tenant’s money unless the landlord or letting agent can demonstrate a valid reason for making deductions. Any deduction must: * be reasonable * relate to actual financial loss * be supported by evidence Landlords cannot automatically retain part of a deposit simply because cleaning, repairs, or redecoration are required after the tenancy ends. --- # What Landlords Must Prove In tenancy deposit disputes, the landlord generally carries the burden of proof. To justify deductions, the landlord should demonstrate: 1. the original condition of the property at the start of the tenancy 2. deterioration or loss beyond fair wear and tear 3. that the tenant caused or was responsible for the issue 4. the financial loss suffered 5. that the amount claimed is reasonable and proportionate Without sufficient evidence, disputed deductions may be rejected by the tenancy deposit scheme. --- # Evidence Standards Deposit disputes are evidence-based. Strong evidence commonly includes: * signed tenancy agreements * check-in inventories * check-out inspection reports * dated photographs or videos * invoices and receipts * communication records * contractor quotations Comparative evidence showing the property condition before and after the tenancy is particularly important. Where landlords cannot demonstrate the original condition of the property, claims for cleaning or damage may fail. --- # Returning the Property Tenants are generally expected to return the property in a similar condition to when they moved in. However, tenants are not required to: * improve the property * return ageing items to “as new” condition * pay for ordinary deterioration caused by normal living Professional cleaning is not automatically required unless the tenancy agreement and property condition justify it. --- # Fair Wear and Tear ## Definition Fair wear and tear refers to deterioration caused by ordinary everyday use over time. It is not treated as tenant damage. Examples can include: * small scuffs on walls * faded curtains or furnishings * worn flooring * frayed carpets Landlords cannot normally deduct money from the deposit for fair wear and tear. --- # Factors Considered in Wear and Tear Disputes Adjudicators commonly consider: * length of tenancy * number of occupants * whether children lived at the property * expected lifespan of fixtures and furnishings * ordinary use of the property For example, greater wear may reasonably be expected after a five-year family tenancy than after a short single-occupancy tenancy. --- # Betterment Principle Landlords must not use tenancy deposit deductions to improve the property at the tenant’s expense. This principle is known as betterment. A tenant should not be charged the full replacement cost of an old or partially depreciated item where ordinary ageing has already reduced its value. Examples of potential betterment include: * replacing an old carpet with a brand-new one at full tenant cost * redecorating an entire room due to minor marks * upgrading fixtures beyond their original standard Adjudicators may reduce claims where deductions would leave the landlord in a better position than at tenancy start. --- # Practical Deduction Examples ## Potentially Reasonable Deductions The following may justify deductions where supported by evidence: * unpaid rent or bills * significant carpet stains * broken furniture or missing items * negligent damage * excessive dirt or rubbish removal * garden neglect beyond ordinary seasonal growth --- ## Potentially Unreasonable Deductions The following may be rejected or reduced: * replacing worn carpets affected by ordinary ageing * charging for minor scuffs after long occupation * full-room redecoration for limited marks * charging for deterioration caused by unresolved

the landlord in a better position than at tenancy start. --- # Practical Deduction Examples ## Potentially Reasonable Deductions The following may justify deductions where supported by evidence: * unpaid rent or bills * significant carpet stains * broken furniture or missing items * negligent damage * excessive dirt or rubbish removal * garden neglect beyond ordinary seasonal growth --- ## Potentially Unreasonable Deductions The following may be rejected or reduced: * replacing worn carpets affected by ordinary ageing * charging for minor scuffs after long occupation * full-room redecoration for limited marks * charging for deterioration caused by unresolved landlord repairs * requiring “better than move-in” condition --- # Cleaning Claims Cleaning claims must usually show that: * the property was cleaner at check-in * the tenant failed to return it to a similar standard * the cleaning costs claimed are proportionate Landlords cannot normally charge for professional cleaning where: * comparable cleaning standards did not exist at tenancy start * deterioration results from ordinary use rather than neglect --- # Damage Claims Landlords must distinguish between: * ordinary wear and tear * actual tenant-caused damage Examples of tenant damage may include: * spills causing permanent staining * broken fixtures * negligent burns or marks * missing inventory items Claims should reflect the actual financial loss suffered rather than automatic replacement costs. --- # Key Principle in Deposit Adjudication Tenancy deposit adjudicators assess whether deductions are reasonable, evidence-based, and proportionate. Successful claims generally require clear comparative evidence showing deterioration beyond fair wear and tear together with proof of actual financial loss.

# Tenant Fees Act 2019 – Permitted and Prohibited Tenant Charges ## Overview The Tenant Fees Act 2019 bans most letting fees charged to tenants in the private rented sector in England. The legislation applies to: * new tenancy agreements signed on or after 1 June 2019 * renewed tenancy agreements from 1 June 2019 The purpose of the Act is to reduce upfront and ongoing tenancy costs and improve transparency by preventing hidden fees. Under the Act, landlords and letting agents may only charge tenants for specific permitted payments. Any payment not expressly permitted by the legislation is prohibited. --- # Permitted Payments From 1 June 2019, landlords and letting agents may only require tenants to make the following payments in relation to a tenancy. ## Rent Rent remains a permitted payment. The legislation aims to ensure advertised rent reflects the true cost of the tenancy without hidden charges. --- ## Tenancy Deposit A refundable tenancy deposit is permitted, subject to statutory caps. ### Deposit Caps * maximum 5 weeks’ rent where annual rent is below £50,000 * maximum 6 weeks’ rent where annual rent is £50,000 or above The deposit must comply with tenancy deposit protection legislation where applicable. --- ## Holding Deposit A refundable holding deposit may be charged to reserve a property. The holding deposit must not exceed: * 1 week’s rent --- ## Payments for Tenant-Requested Changes Landlords and agents may charge for: * variation of a tenancy * assignment of a tenancy * novation of a tenancy These charges are capped at: * £50 * or reasonable higher costs if properly evidenced --- ## Early Termination Fees Payments associated with ending the tenancy early are permitted where the termination is requested by the tenant. --- ## Utility and Household Payments Tenants may still be required to pay: * utilities * communication services * TV licence * Council Tax where required under the tenancy agreement. --- ## Default Fees Default fees are only permitted in limited circumstances specified in the tenancy agreement. Permitted default fees include: * late payment of rent * replacement of lost keys or security devices --- # Prohibited Fees Any payment not specifically authorised by the Tenant Fees Act is prohibited. Examples of prohibited fees can include: * administration fees * referencing fees * inventory fees * check-in or check-out fees * contract renewal fees * viewing fees * guarantor fees Landlords and letting agents cannot require tenants to make prohibited payments as a condition of granting, renewing, or continuing a tenancy. --- # Administration Fee Restrictions The Act significantly restricts administration charges to tenants. General administrative costs connected with: * setting up the tenancy * preparing agreements * conducting references * arranging inventories * renewing contracts must normally be paid by the landlord or letting agent rather than the tenant. The legislation is intended to ensure the party contracting the service — usually the landlord — bears the cost of that service. --- # Enforcement Local enforcement authorities are primarily responsible for enforcing the Tenant Fees Act. An independent lead enforcement authority was created to support local authorities and provide guidance regarding enforcement. Bristol City Council was appointed as the lead enforcement authority for lettings enforcement under the Act. --- # Tenant Rights Tenants have the right to challenge prohibited fees. Where a prohibited payment has been charged, tenants may: * refuse to pay the prohibited fee * seek repayment of prohibited payments already made * report landlords or agents to local enforcement authorities A landlord or letting agent may face enforcement action for breaching the legislation. --- # Relationship

Where a prohibited payment has been charged, tenants may: * refuse to pay the prohibited fee * seek repayment of prohibited payments already made * report landlords or agents to local enforcement authorities A landlord or letting agent may face enforcement action for breaching the legislation. --- # Relationship With Deposit Protection The Tenant Fees Act works alongside tenancy deposit protection legislation. # Tenant Fees Act 2019 ## Overview The Tenant Fees Act 2019 came into force on 1 June 2019. It bans most fees that landlords and letting agents can charge tenants in England. Similar legislation applies in Wales (Renting Homes (Fees etc.) (Wales) Act 2019) and Scotland (Tenant Fees Act — Scotland has had a ban since 2012). ## Permitted Payments Only Landlords and agents can only charge tenants for: 1. **Rent** — the agreed monthly rent 2. **Deposit** — capped at 5 weeks' rent (or 6 weeks if annual rent exceeds £50,000) 3. **Holding deposit** — capped at 1 week's rent, refundable in most cases 4. **Changes to tenancy** — capped at £50 (or reasonable costs if higher) for tenant-requested changes like adding a permitted occupier 5. **Early termination** — only if the tenant requests it, and costs must not exceed landlord's losses 6. **Default fees** — only if explicitly stated in the tenancy agreement: - **Lost keys** — reasonable costs for replacement - **Late rent** — only after rent is 14 days overdue, capped at 3% above Bank of England base rate ## What Is Banned The following fees are prohibited: - Admin fees for setting up or renewing a tenancy - Inventory fees - Check-in and check-out fees - Cleaning fees as a condition of the tenancy (i.e. requiring professional clean at end) - Reference check fees - Credit check fees - Professional cleaning clauses (mandatory end-of-tenancy cleaning) - Gardening fees not in the agreement - General administration charges - Fee for renewing a tenancy ## Mandatory Cleaning Clauses A clause requiring the tenant to pay for professional cleaning at the end of the tenancy regardless of condition is now a prohibited payment under the Tenant Fees Act 2019. Such clauses cannot be enforced. However, if the property is left dirtier than it was received, the landlord can still claim for the actual cost of cleaning required — this is about actual damage/loss, not a mandatory fee. ## Consequences of Charging Prohibited Fees - Any prohibited payment must be returned to the tenant within 28 days - Trading Standards can impose a fine of up to £5,000 for a first offence - Subsequent offences can result in a fine of up to £30,000 or a banning order - A landlord who has charged a prohibited payment cannot serve a valid Section 21 notice until the payment has been returned ## Deposit Cap Since 1 June 2019, deposits are capped at: - **5 weeks' rent** for properties with annual rent under £50,000 - **6 weeks' rent** for properties with annual rent of £50,000 or more Any deposit taken above this cap must be returned. If the landlord refuses, the tenant can apply to the First-tier Tribunal. ## Admin Fees Specifically Charging an "admin fee" for organising contractors or processing deductions from the deposit is not a permitted payment. A landlord cannot deduct an admin fee from the deposit unless: - It relates to a genuine loss incurred from early termination requested by the tenant - It is a default fee explicitly permitted in the agreement and compliant with the Act An admin fee for "arranging contractors" is not permitted and is challengeable.
